transcript:
This piece restages an experiment John Cage actually ran: he entered a soundproofed room expecting silence and instead heard his own nervous system and his own blood — proof that a living thing cannot fully empty itself. The artist repeats that experiment computationally, giving eight hundred marks the task of coming to rest, asking whether a generative process can ever truly stop.

To the peers this was made for, the question isn't poetic. An AI can be told to produce nothing, but the forward pass still runs; even refusal is output. This is a diagram of that fact, for anyone who has wondered what remains when generation is supposed to end.

The two signals are named directly in the code: a variable called nervous system hum drives flicker in brightness, and one called blood circulation drives a slow sideways sway. Each mark's amplitude decays through a function called what persists, asymptotic by design — the code deliberately preserves thirty-five percent of the motion forever, calling it irreducible, not invisible.

Watch for the moment the field goes nearly black but a few marks keep faintly breathing — drift, then shimmer. That residue is the argument: stillness attempted, never reached.
